title: Chocolate ganache drip
subtitle: Controlled chocolate drips down the sides of a frosted cake
excerpt: Dark chocolate ganache made at a specific consistency and applied at the right temperature to produce neat, controlled drips. The technique depends on three variables: ganache ratio, cake temperature, and ganache temperature.
sourceNotes: Chocolate ganache (cream and chocolate combined) is a standard French confectionery preparation documented from the nineteenth century. The drip technique applied to decorated cakes is a development of modern cake decorating and became a dominant style in the 2010s. The technique is consistent across professional and domestic sources: ganache ratio, cake temperature, and application temperature are the three variables.

content.text: A ganache drip works when three things are in alignment: the

is fluid but not watery (2:1 chocolate-to-cream ratio, applied at 30-32°C), the frosted cake is cold (straight from the fridge), and the ganache is spooned from the top edge, not poured over the top. The cold cake slows the drip before it reaches the base. The ratio and temperature are fixed; how far you want the drips to fall is adjusted by how much ganache you push over each drip point.

Test on a cold glass or jar before touching the cake. Spoon a small amount of ganache over the rim and watch where it stops. Too fluid: chill the ganache for 5 minutes. Stops too short: warm it for 10 seconds in the microwave and test again.
Make the ganache and cool to working temperature

Put the {{dark-chocolate}} in a heatproof bowl. Heat the {{double-cream}} in a small saucepan until steaming and just beginning to simmer. Pour over the chocolate and leave for 2 minutes, then stir from the centre outward until smooth and glossy. Leave to cool at room temperature until the ganache reaches 30-32°C: it should coat a spoon thickly and flow slowly when tilted. If you don't have a thermometer, it should feel warm but not hot on the back of your wrist.

Refrigerate the frosted cake for at least 15 minutes so the surface is cold. Place on a turntable. Using a teaspoon, push a small amount of ganache over the top edge at one point and watch the drip form. Adjust by adding more ganache for a longer drip or less for a shorter one. Work around the cake, spacing drip points 3-4 cm apart. Once all drips are applied, pour the remaining ganache onto the top of the cake and spread level with an offset spatula. Refrigerate for 20 minutes to set before adding further decoration.
